fields.description:
## Dependencies (STACKED — read against the stack tip, not origin/main)
e165 builds directly on two ANCESTOR changes in this review stack that are NOT yet
merged to origin/main: **ddbe** (identity entity + the `rebar identity` `main()`
intercept in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`) and **bde5** (`src/rebar/attest/authorship.py`
with `sign_authorship`/`verify_authorship`/`resolve_trust_root`). Both exist at
e165's implementation base (the stack tip). Review this plan with `--source local`
so those ancestors are visible; against origin/main alone they will (correctly)
appear absent.

## Context
Give an `identity`'s public keys a LIFECYCLE with cryptographic continuity:
a trust-on-first-use (TOFU) genesis key, and subsequent key add/rotate/revoke
changes that must each be signed by a currently-valid key of that identity (fills
git-bug's unimplemented `IsProtected`). A key's validity is scoped to its ERA —
its position in the authoritative MERGED event log — never to an attacker-set
event timestamp. Builds on ddbe (identity + `keys`) and bde5 (authorship
sign/verify via `rebar.authorship.v1`).

## Why
Without a signed rotation chain, anyone who can append a KEY event to an identity
could inject their own key and forge authorship. Anchoring validity to merged-log
position (not wall-clock) stops an attacker from backdating a forged event into a
revoked key's window.

## What
### 1. KEY lifecycle events on the identity (TAG_DELTA precedent)
Add key-management event types folded by the reducer into an era-scoped keyring:
- Event types (new constants in `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py`, added to the
  known-event-type set; bump the reducer version so older clones
  preserve-and-ignore — see the TAG_DELTA v3 note): `KEY_ADD` and `KEY_REVOKE`
  (a rotate = a `KEY_REVOKE` of the old key + a `KEY_ADD` of the new, or a single
  `KEY_ADD` that supersedes; keep the two primitive events, model rotate as their
  composition).
- `KEY_ADD` data: `{public_key: <authorized-keys line>, signature: <DSSE envelope
  or null for genesis>}`. `KEY_REVOKE` data: `{public_key: <line>, signature:
  <DSSE envelope>}`.
- New reducer processor `process_key_event` (in `_processors.py`, dispatched from
  `_replay.py`) folds these into `state["keyring"]`: a list of
  `{public_key, added_epoch, revoked_epoch|null}` records, where `epoch` is the ordinal
  position (see §2). `state["keys"]` (from ddbe) becomes the DERIVED set of keys
  whose validity window contains the CURRENT (latest) epoch — i.e. currently-valid
  keys — so existing readers keep working.

  SEEDING + MIGRATION (required): (a) `src/rebar/reducer/_state.py`'s
  `make_initial_state()` must seed `keyring = []` and `keyring_epoch = 0` (as it
  seeds `managed_refs`/`claimed_session`), so a ticket that never had a KEY event
  has canonical zero-values, not absent keys. (b) BOOTSTRAP for identities created
  by ddbe: `process_create` for an `identity` whose CREATE carried a static `keys`
  list seeds an implicit GENESIS keyring — one `{public_key, added_epoch:0,
  revoked_epoch:null}` record per CREATE key (all genesis keys share epoch 0 — the
  genesis is ONE lifecycle step regardless of key count), and sets the cursor
  `keyring_epoch = 1` (epoch 0 consumed by genesis; the next real KEY_ADD/KEY_REVOKE
  gets epoch 1). So a pre-existing identity's `keys` are all valid from epoch 0 and
  `resolve_trust_root`/`verify_authorship` work with no explicit KEY event. An
  identity created with NO keys has an empty keyring and `keyring_epoch = 0` (genesis
  happens later, at the first KEY_ADD, which is then the TOFU epoch-0 step).

### 2. Era = a snapshot-safe keyring EPOCH counter (NOT event timestamp, NOT the raw replay loop index)
Maintain a monotonic `state["keyring_epoch"]` integer = the NEXT epoch to assign
(a cursor, not a per-key id). Each key-lifecycle STEP consumes one epoch: the
reducer records the change at the CURRENT `keyring_epoch`, THEN increments it by 1.
MULTIPLE keys may be valid at the same epoch (a keyring legitimately holds several
concurrently-valid keys); an epoch does not uniquely own one key — it is only a
validity-window boundary. A key added at epoch `a` and
revoked at epoch `r` is VALID for epochs in `[a, r)` (open at `r`: a key cannot
sign at/after its own revocation); a never-revoked key has `revoked_epoch = None`.

CRITICAL — snapshot safety (the raw replay loop index is WRONG): `replay_events()`
starts from the latest SNAPSHOT and skips pre-snapshot events, so the loop index is
NOT the absolute ordinal. Therefore: (a) `process_snapshot` FREEZES `keyring_epoch`
(and the full `keyring`) into the SNAPSHOT `compiled_state` and RESTORES them on
replay; (b) `process_key_event` increments the RESTORED `keyring_epoch` counter, not
any loop index. This keeps epochs stable across compaction.

Epoch is derived purely from the canonical fold ORDER (the reducer's HLC-filename
replay order — identical on every clone after sync, see reducer/_cache.py,
_processors.py:538), NEVER from the attacker-controllable `data`-level timestamp.

Canonical signed content of a KEY op (what the authorship signature covers): the
epoch is NOT signed (it is assigned at fold time). The signature covers
`canonical.canonical_str({"op": "KEY_ADD" | "KEY_REVOKE", "identity_id": <id>,
"public_key": <authorized-keys line>}).encode("utf-8")` (sorted-keys canonical
form from `rebar._store.canonical`). This binds a specific key operation to a
specific identity; the write-gate checks it was authorized by a currently-valid key
(the trust root at the latest epoch BEFORE this op).

### 3. Write-time refusal of unsigned/invalidly-signed KEY changes
A `KEY_ADD`/`KEY_REVOKE` after genesis is REFUSED (the write raises, no event is
appended) unless its `signature` is a valid `rebar.authorship.v1` authorship
envelope (bde5 `verify_authorship`) over the KEY event's canonical content,
produced by a key that is CURRENTLY valid for this identity (valid at the era
immediately preceding this change). The GENESIS `KEY_ADD` (the first key event for
an identity that has no keys yet) is TOFU: accepted WITHOUT a signature (nothing to
chain to). Surface this via a new command/library seam
`add_identity_key` / `revoke_identity_key` (CLI `rebar identity key add/revoke`)
that performs the signature check before appending.

### 4. Epoch-scoped authorship verification (extends bde5)
Add `verify_authorship_at_epoch(envelope, identity_id, epoch, *, repo_root=None) ->
Verdict`: resolve the identity's keyring, build the trust root from ONLY the keys
whose `[added_epoch, revoked_epoch)` window contains `epoch`, and verify. An event whose
signing key was revoked before the event's epoch fails; one within the key's valid
epoch passes. (`verify_authorship` from bde5 stays as the "current-era"
convenience.)

Epoch derivation for an arbitrary (non-KEY) event: the epoch that GOVERNS an
event's authorship is the number of the identity's KEY lifecycle events whose
canonical fold position precedes that event. Provide a helper
`epoch_for_position(identity_id, event_position, *, repo_root=None) -> int`.
It must be SNAPSHOT-AWARE (KEY_ADD/KEY_REVOKE are KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES and are folded
into the identity's SNAPSHOT by compact.py, whose files are then retired — so a
naive file scan under-counts). Compute it by folding the identity's keyring the same
way the reducer does — start from the latest SNAPSHOT's restored `keyring_epoch`
(the epoch as of compaction) and add only the POST-snapshot KEY events whose
canonical `{HLC-ts}-{uuid}` position precedes `event_position`. Equivalently: reduce
the identity's KEY history (SNAPSHOT base + live KEY events) truncated at
`event_position` and read the resulting `keyring_epoch`. Reuse the reducer's
snapshot-start logic (do not re-scan raw files independently). e165 defines this derivation + the
`verify_authorship_at_epoch` primitive (which takes the resolved epoch); WIRING it
into live event verification at the write/merge path is 3183's job. The AC tests
call `verify_authorship_at_epoch` with explicit epochs on a constructed keyring, so
the primitive is exercised independent of the derivation helper.

### 5. Write-path allowlist + CLI wiring (grounded consumer list)
- `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`: `KEY_ADD`/`KEY_REVOKE` MUST be added to the
  hardcoded `EVENT_TYPES` frozenset (~line 50) or `_validate_event` raises
  a `StoreError` (invalid event_type; ~line 120) and the write seam fails before any
  event lands. Separate from the reducer known-type set in `_version.py` — BOTH need
  the new types (the TAG_DELTA precedent touched both).
- `src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py`: bump `_REDUCER_CACHE_VERSION` so stale `.cache.json`
  files (which lack `keyring`/`keyring_epoch`) are invalidated.
- `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py`: `KEY_ADD`/`KEY_REVOKE` must be placed in one of
  its orphan-type frozensets (`_AUTO_RECOVER_ORPHAN_TYPES` or
  `_HUMAN_TRIAGE_ORPHAN_TYPES`) — a test asserts these together cover every
  KNOWN_EVENT_TYPE except CREATE/SNAPSHOT. KEY events onto an identity are not
  orphans in the graph sense; classify them under human-triage (or a dedicated
  exempt path) so the coverage assertion holds.
- CLI `rebar identity key add|revoke`: extend the identity subcommand `main()`
  intercept in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (the seam ddbe used) — NOT the
  `_ARGV_REGISTRY`/`_WRITES_FULL` golden-file path (which would touch `tests/golden`).

## Scope
In: the KEY_ADD/KEY_REVOKE events + reducer keyring fold + epoch computation, the
signed-rotation write gate (add/revoke seam), TOFU genesis, and
`verify_authorship_at_epoch`. Out: auto-signing every normal event / the merge-gate
enforcement (3183), Jira mapping (264f/2f13). Key material lives in `keys` as
before; private keys are operator-held (signatures are produced out-of-band via the
bde5 signing seam).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A `KEY_ADD`/`KEY_REVOKE` on an identity that already has a genesis key is
  REFUSED unless accompanied by a valid authorship signature from a currently-valid
  key of that identity; a test asserts an UNSIGNED key-add is refused (raises / no
  event appended) while the genesis key-add (first key) is accepted TOFU.
- [ ] An event whose authorship envelope was produced by a key REVOKED at an
  earlier era fails `verify_authorship_at_epoch` for that event's epoch, while an event
  signed within the key's valid era PASSES (test builds a keyring with an add then
  a revoke and checks both sides of the boundary).
- [ ] Validity is computed from the keyring EPOCH (fold order), NOT the event's
  `data` timestamp: a test with a misleading/backdated `data` timestamp does NOT
  change which epoch (key set) governs it.
- [ ] Snapshot safety: an identity whose keyring events are COMPACTED into a
  SNAPSHOT reduces to the SAME keyring + `keyring_epoch` as the uncompacted log,
  and a KEY event ADDED AFTER the snapshot gets the correct next epoch (a test
  compacts then adds a key and asserts the epoch is contiguous, not reset).

## file_impact
- `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` (KEY_ADD/KEY_REVOKE constants + version bump + known-type set)
- `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` (process_key_event: fold keyring + epoch counter + derive current keys; process_snapshot freezes/restores keyring+keyring_epoch)
- `src/rebar/reducer/_replay.py` (dispatch KEY events)
- `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` (verify_authorship_at_epoch + era-scoped trust root)
- `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py` (add_identity_key/revoke_identity_key seam with the signed-rotation write gate; CLI wiring)
